raw_consumer_statement:
On XX/XX/XXXX, Citibank alerted me to a potentially fraudulent transaction on one of my credit cards. This transaction was for tickets to a XXXX XXXX in XXXX, and I confirmed that neither I nor any users of my card authorized this purchase. At the same time they alerted me to 4 other suspicious transactions, all involving tickets to concerts or shows, in various cities ( XXXX XXXX XXXX, XXXX, XXXX ). I told Citibank that none of these charges was authorized and they removed them from my account. 

A few weeks later, I received a letter from Citibank indication that two of the merchants ( XXXX in XXXX and XXXX XXXX in XXXX XXXX disputed the charge back, and said the transactions appeared valid to them, and the tickets had been used. Citibank gave me the opportunity to affirm in writing that these transactions were fraudulent, which I did. I pointed out that the email addresses to which the digital tickets were sent were unknown to me, and were different email addresses used at each of these merchants. 

On XX/XX/XXXX, Citibank wrote to me to say that they agree with the merchant that there was no fraud, and I was responsible for the two charges, totaling {$1000.00}, and that their investigation was closed. 

Citibank refuses to hear more evidence and is requiring me to pay these charges or they will report it to the credit bureaus. 

To me the evidence is clear : there were a series of transactions using my card number ( but no physical card or chip was presented ) in different cities around the world for concerts and shows. They agree that 4 charges of these were fraud, but somehow insist that another 2 were valid simply because the merchant says so. Moreover, Citibank alerted me to the fraud in the first place.
